flashcards.Back: The central authority responsible for managing a pool of IP addresses and automatically assigning them to clients along with other configuration parameters.
A network device configured to automatically request and receive its IP address and other network settings from a DHCP server upon connecting.
A router feature that intercepts local DHCP broadcast messages and forwards them as unicast to a central DHCP server on a different subnet.
The first step in the DHCP process (broadcast), where a client searches for any available DHCP servers on the network.
The second step where a server responds to a 'Discover' message with a proposed IP address and configuration for the client.
The third step where the client informs the server that it accepts the offered IP address and network parameters.
The final step where the server confirms the lease and provides the final configuration details to the client.
The duration for which a client is permitted to use an assigned IP address. The client must renew the lease before it expires to keep the address.
The Cisco interface command used to enable DHCP relay functionality by specifying the IP address of the remote DHCP server.
A configuration on the DHCP server that prevents certain IP addresses from being assigned to clients, typically reserved for static use on routers or servers.
The network service used to resolve human-readable hostnames (like www.cisco.com) into numerical IP addresses.
The sequence of queries a client or server performs to find the IP address associated with a specific domain name.
The standard DNS record used to map a hostname to a 32-bit IPv4 address.
The DNS record type used to map a hostname to a 128-bit IPv6 address.
Canonical Name record. A DNS entry that acts as an alias, pointing one hostname to another hostname.
A query where the client asks its local DNS server to handle the entire resolution process and return the final answer.
A query where a DNS server responds with the best information it has (typically a referral to another server) instead of resolving the whole name itself.
The primary transport port used for standard DNS queries and replies because of its low overhead.
The port used by DNS for large data transfers such as zone transfers between DNS servers.
A local storage area on a host that keeps track of recently resolved domain names to speed up future access to the same sites.
A named group of addresses and parameters defined on a Cisco router configured as a DHCP server.
An error that occurs when two devices on the same network are assigned the same IP address. DHCP servers check for this using ping before assignment.
The Cisco command used to view the list of currently leased IP addresses and their associated client MAC addresses.
A verification command that displays statistics about configured DHCP pools, including utilization and available addresses.
A configuration on a local DNS server that instructs it to send queries it cannot resolve to a specific external DNS server.
Fully Qualified Domain Name. The complete domain name for a specific host on the internet (e.g., mail.google.com.).
The process of resolving an IP address back into its associated hostname using a specialized DNS record (PTR).
A specialized DHCP configuration parameter often used to provide the IP addresses of TFTP servers to IP phones.
Name Server record. A DNS entry that identifies which servers are authoritative for a particular domain.
A DNS server that holds the master copies of the records for a specific domain and can provide definitive answers to queries.
